posts.name: Michael Cromwell
posts.cooked:
For those of you who have had the wine and have also tried Beta (which also released today), how would you compare them? I have had some Beta, but have not had the pleasure of trying any Macdonald – though I do have one bottle of 2016 which I think I may drink right before I die, if I understand the aging potential of these wines.

I’ll try and answer this though I expect board opinions to vary. On a scale of restraint, it goes Vare, Montecillo, MacDonald, with MacDonald being opulent in comparison, but by no means opulent by today’s Napa standards. Same order for fruit notes. I’ve used the term ‘thinking your way through a bottle of wine’ to describe wines that are complex and that applies to each of these though for different reasons. Vare would go well with food but not steaks, the other two could handle steaks and anything else reds went with (within reason). For my palate MacDonald is king in comparison but let’s be clear—that Montecillo is a no slouch. I bought 6 Montecillos today—it’s that good. MacDonald achieves greatness with heavenly fruit, Ketan is seemingly more of an artist, doing more with less. Hope this helps—

hey pete!

Step 1 - use classic CT (i’m doing it on a PC)
Step 2 - identify the wine you’re looking for. I chose the 2018 MacDonald Cabernet Sauvignon.
Step 3 - once you’ve landed on the wine’s “product” page, simply scroll all the way to the bottom.
Step 4 - ensure that “Community Holdings” isn’t hidden
Step 5 - browse the buying habits of everyone that’s logged the wine into their cellar
Step 6 - say it with me, “the mets suck!”

posts.name: Andrew A r n t f i e l d
posts.cooked:
I don’t think it’s “everybody.” I think it’s everybody whose cellars are public, right?

Correct.

Also, you don’t need to be in Classic view on CT.

If you use the current interface, open the page of the wine in question (e.g. 2018 MacDonald Cabernet Sauvignon), and towards the bottom of the right column you’ll see “Community Holdings”. You can click Friends, Community or Stores.

If you click Community, you’ll see very detailed info about everyone (whose cellars are public) who has the wine, including their handle, number of bottles, date of purchase and source. You’ll also see which bottles are pending delivery vs. in the cellar, and you’ll be able to determine who has been drinking the wine (bottles purchased vs. bottles still in cellar).

posts.name: Jon Lawrence
posts.cooked: I think CT lists all bottles in the community holdings totals regardless of whether a cellar is public or private. To test this, I added a fictional 30L bottle of 2018 MacDonald to my cellar, which is private. When you search 2018 MacDonald and look at the community holdings on the right, it lists 756 bottles, which includes 709 pending, 38 in cellars and 9 consumed. The community holdings do not differentiate by bottle size as far as I could tell. To figure out the bottles by size I ran an advanced report for 2018 MacDonald and selected bottle size as one of the qualifying parameters. I ran the report 3 times, once each for 750ML, 1.5L and 30L, and the bottle counts were 726, 20 and 1 respectively. That adds up to 747, plus the 9 consumed adds to 756 which ties back to the community holdings total. I just deleted my fictional 30L and replaced it with my 3 750’s, so total bottles is now 758. If I logout and search 2018 MacDonald, I can still the community holdings totals, just not the private cellars detail. I can still run the advanced reports while logged out to find counts by bottle size.

The release mailer said that 500 cases were produced, so even at 758 bottles of which only 20 are mags and 1 is Mike’s 3L , only a fraction of the total bottles produced have been entered into cellars so far.

“ Dear Friends and Family,

My brother and I are humbled by the interest we have received during this year’s release. As a result, our 2018 MACDONALD Oakville Cabernet Sauvignon has sold out. We appreciate everyone’s support of our small family vineyard and we will continue to create wines that express our site and old vines. posts.name: Andrew Taylor
posts.cooked:
So, those of you who have been buying these since the beginning - is it worthwhile opening a bottle when they arrive to see what they are about?

I recently asked Alex about vintages to open and his reply was the 2014 is currently the best to try with a 6-8 hour decant.

posts.name: David Baum
posts.cooked:
So, those of you who have been buying these since the beginning - is it worthwhile opening a bottle when they arrive to see what they are about?

I recently asked Alex about vintages to open and his reply was the 2014 is currently the best to try with a 6-8 hour decant.

Id likely wait a couple months and then give it a nice long decant (12-24 hours). If you never had it, you should try one. you;ll get more in the years ahead. Just dont be one of the guys who has all 3 bottles going back many vintages who’ll one day look at secondary prices and sell it all without ever drinking any of theirs while drinking their friends. You know who you are out there

I visited Anton several months ago up in Chico on a college tour with my daughter. I mailed him a 15 MacDonald 2 weeks in advance so it would be settled and he could decant it. When I arrived he had decanted it for about 10 hours along with 8 other high end 15’s all served double blind. I was a little surprised but went with the flow. I had never had it blind before nor heard of anyone having it blind. It was the unanimous crowd favorite by a good margin over some exceptional wines

Here’s a link to my post on that night

WineBerserkers – 12 Apr 21

Saturday Night at Anton's with MacDonald and Friends

Just got back from a college visit to Chico with daughter and ex. I figured it was an opportunity to meet Anton in person and pull some corks. He offered to provide the wines. Knowing he had never had a MacDonald I offered to bring one. He accepted...

Reading time: 5 mins 🕑 Likes: 18 ❤

link_counts.title: Saturday Night at Anton's with MacDonald and Friends - WINE TALK - WineBerserkers

posts.name: mike pobega
posts.cooked:
A thing of true beauty: MACDONALD old vine Cabernet Sauvignon week(s) before harvest time.
Pinhole camera image.

Excellent photo. Thanks for sharing.

Assuming that is a 50+ year-old vine, any chance the grapes from that vine could have been used to make the Monday Reserves from the 70’s?

there are 47 year old vines and a special plot planted by the Macdonald brothers grandfather from 1954 so most definitely. Once Mondavi started the Reserve program, I would believe he used that fruit.
Interesting read:

guildsomm.com

The True Story of To-Kalon Vineyard

Prior to the turn of the 20th Century, there was a winery in Napa Valley which used the name ‘Tokalon’… That winery was sold off in parcels during the first fifteen to twenty years of the 20th Century and use of the name was discon...

link_counts.title: The True Story of To-Kalon Vineyard - Matt Stamp - Articles - GuildSomm

posts.name: Curtis Fong

posts.name: J a y H a c k
posts.cooked:
So, those of you who have been buying these since the beginning - is it worthwhile opening a bottle when they arrive to see what they are about?

I open for special occasions, as do some of my friends, but the wine is really too new to have sufficient history. Compare with Scarecrow, that I have been buying since the 2005 vintage. I am making my way through the Scarecrow by opening one per vintage for about the past 8 or 10 years. At special occasions. Night before son leaves to go to law school. My mother’s shiva to celebrate her life (she preferred Aubert chard so we opened one of those). My 70th Birthday. You get the idea. I am starting to do that with MacDonald also. But it’s just not a Thursday night pizza wine because they are so rare.
posts.display_username: J a y H a c k

posts.name: Nick Rutk0wski
posts.cooked:
So, those of you who have been buying these since the beginning - is it worthwhile opening a bottle when they arrive to see what they are about?

I’d hide them for 5-6 years at the very least. Even then you probably need a 12 hour decant to really get the whole picture. I’ve been able to grab 15/16/18s and won’t open for a while.

posts.name: David Baum
posts.cooked:
So, those of you who have been buying these since the beginning - is it worthwhile opening a bottle when they arrive to see what they are about?

I open for special occasions, as do some of my friends, but the wine is really too new to have sufficient history. Compare with Scarecrow, that I have been buying since the 2005 vintage. I am making my way through the Scarecrow by opening one per vintage for about the past 8 or 10 years. At special occasions. Night before son leaves to go to law school. My mother’s shiva to celebrate her life (she preferred Aubert chard so we opened one of those). My 70th Birthday. You get the idea. I am starting to do that with MacDonald also. But it’s just not a Thursday night pizza wine because they are so rare.

I look at it differently. It can be a lovely Thursday night pizza wine and for me has zero to do with the occassion. The wine makes the day special not vice versa.

For me it is all about who Im drinking them with not when. Id be thrilled to drink one on a Monday afternoon with my best friend over In N Out burgers. Fortunately he’s on the list now so it could be his bottle as well. Ive drunk 3 vintages out of my cellar and particularly enjoy sharing them with people that havent had them who would appreciate them irrespective of the day. Next up will likely be 13 or 17. So far

2014 - drank Summer 2019 when Pobega was in town. His 1st and I beleive he’s on the list now
2016 - drank Dec 2020 with my two best wine buddies locally. Both are on the list now and now they have to drink theirs with me
2015 - drank Spring 2021 while up in Chico with Anton, the son of one of the two wine buddies above and Anton’s local friends. His 1st.

More then anything Im spreading them out as Ive had them with close to 10 years of age and know what they can become. They will make the day special whenever that is

posts.name: Brian Armston
posts.cooked:
On another note, it was the highest rated wine of the 2018 vintage by WS. I bet these will fetch close to a G a pop in the secondary.

When I told my GF about it the other day, she asked if I was considering it. I told her I would rather break up than do that.

Spent the next two hours trying to convince her that I was joking. Well, maybe I was, maybe I wasn’t

posts.name: David Baum
posts.cooked: That was pretty cool for them. I was up last weekend to catch a show and pick up some wine that’s been sitting at vinfillment too long. I had a free hour to grab couple beers with Alex Friday night before my concert and the WS cover came up. For them it makes them no more money and really doesn’t help the business as they have all they can handle. More than anything it’s a cool moment for them a kin to an athlete landing on the cover of SI. It’s a WTF how did we get here moment. Well deserved

posts.name: Mike Steinberg
posts.cooked:
So, Alex is in town (NY/NJ) for the week for the wine spectator NY wine event. Graeme is one of the speakers at the event. $1495 per person for that Zachys dinner- yikes.

Instead, had Alex over my house for dinner last night, along with a number of my wine spectator forumite/ cellar tracker/ beserkers friends. Alex brought the 18 MacDonald cab- it’s a beast. Only had 3 hours of decant time because The wine had been on a plane most of the day, but it really needs about 24 hrs of decant time at this point. When I had the wine earlier this year in Napa, Alex had given it 12 hours of decant time ahead of dinner, but truthfully, the wine was even better the next morning.

We missed you Bomba- let’s plan a San Diego offline

I look at it differently. It can be a lovely Thursday night pizza wine and for me has zero to do with the occassion. The wine makes the day special not vice versa.

For me it is all about who Im drinking them with not when. Id be thrilled to drink one on a Monday afternoon with my best friend over In N Out burgers. Fortunately he’s on the list now so it could be his bottle as well. Ive drunk 3 vintages out of my cellar and particularly enjoy sharing them with people that havent had them who would appreciate them irrespective of the day. Next up will likely be 13 or 17. So far

2014 - drank Summer 2019 when Pobega was in town. His 1st and I beleive he’s on the list now
2016 - drank Dec 2020 with my two best wine buddies locally. Both are on the list now and now they have to drink theirs with me
2015 - drank Spring 2021 while up in Chico with Anton, the son of one of the two wine buddies above and Anton’s local friends. His 1st.

More then anything Im spreading them out as Ive had them with close to 10 years of age and know what they can become. They will make the day special whenever that is

Taking your lead, we just had a ‘14 tonight with good friends and pizza. It paired spectacularly. 4 hour decant. The most impeccable Napa cab I’ve had, it’s already drinking wonderfully. It was my first vintage of allocation, and I had been holding off on opening, looking for the right moment. Pizza and friends and amazing wine is what it’s all about.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
So, those of you who have been buying these since the beginning - is it worthwhile opening a bottle when they arrive to see what they are about?

I would say the opportunity cost is quite high, especially in a vintage such as 2018, which shows a lot of puppy fat young but which will tighten up in the way the 2012 did with bottle age. Better would be to go to visit the vineyard and bully Graeme into opening a 2011. It takes me two-to-three years of bullying per bottle (you might be more persuasive), but the 2011 really tells you everything you need to know.

posts.name: Scott Brunson
posts.cooked:

I would say the opportunity cost is quite high, especially in a vintage such as 2018, which shows a lot of puppy fat young but which will tighten up in the way the 2012 did with bottle age. We drank ‘15 with Alex a few years ago—young but spectacular.

posts.name: Alan Rath
posts.cooked:
So, those of you who have been buying these since the beginning - is it worthwhile opening a bottle when they arrive to see what they are about?

I would say the opportunity cost is quite high, especially in a vintage such as 2018, which shows a lot of puppy fat young but which will tighten up in the way the 2012 did with bottle age. Better would be to go to visit the vineyard and bully Graeme into opening a 2011. It takes me two-to-three years of bullying per bottle (you might be more persuasive), but the 2011 really tells you everything you need to know.

William, do you not think 2011 is an outlier for most Napa producers? Don’t get me wrong, I personally love the vintage, which seems like an old school throw back to earlier days, like the 70s. But I’m not entirely sure I could judge a producer on the 11 alone.

posts.name: Michael S. Monie
posts.cooked:
But this is why the allocation list moves so slowly. Who would drop off the list when you can just 5x your money by waiting a few months?

I did. Received 3 bottles for each of 2011, 2012, and 2013 and then stopped. No reflection on the wines because I haven’t drunk any of them; I generally like Napa Cabs with at least 10 years of bottle aging. Also dropped off of VHR and Greer that year. Stayed with Roy Piper from the beginning through the 2016 vintage.

excerpt from Recent WS roundtable discussion:

question to Andy Erickson: what is a cabernet not made by you that opened your eyes recently?

The MacDonald Brothers’ wines, To Kalon Vineyard, love those wines, old vines, pure classic style, cool to see a younger generation coming in and putting a flag in the ground
